name: Set Up a Python Project with Virtual Environment
description: Create an isolated Python environment with dependencies, linting, and a reproducible project structure ready for collaboration.
trendingReason: Virtual environments remain the foundation of every Python project and are required knowledge for any Python developer.

steps.title: Create the project directory
steps.description: Create a new directory for your project and navigate into it.
steps.tip: Use a descriptive name that matches your package or application name.

steps.title: Create a virtual environment
steps.description: Creates an isolated Python environment in the .venv directory. This keeps project dependencies separate from your system Python.
steps.tip: The .venv name is a widely adopted convention. Most editors and tools auto-detect it.

steps.title: Activate the environment
steps.description: Activates the virtual environment so pip and python commands use the isolated copy. Your shell prompt will change to indicate the active environment.
steps.warning: You must activate the environment in every new terminal session. Forgetting to activate is the most common cause of 'module not found' errors.

steps.title: Upgrade pip and install dependencies
steps.command: pip install --upgrade pip && pip install requests flask
steps.description: Upgrade pip to the latest version and install your project dependencies. Always upgrade pip first to avoid compatibility warnings.

steps.title: Freeze dependencies to requirements.txt
steps.description: Lock all installed packages and their exact versions into a requirements file. This ensures reproducible installs across machines and CI.
steps.tip: Run this command every time you add or update a dependency to keep the file current.

steps.title: Add .venv to .gitignore
steps.description: Exclude the virtual environment directory from version control. Each developer should create their own .venv from requirements.txt.
steps.warning: Never commit the .venv directory. It contains platform-specific binaries that will not work on other machines.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Python project setup with virtual environment
set -e

PROJECT_NAME=${1:-myproject}
mkdir -p "$PROJECT_NAME" && cd "$PROJECT_NAME"

python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

pip install --upgrade pip
pip install requests flask pytest ruff

pip freeze > requirements.txt

echo '.venv/' > .gitignore
echo '__pycache__/' >> .gitignore
echo '*.pyc' >> .gitignore
echo '.pytest_cache/' >> .gitignore

mkdir -p src tests
touch src/__init__.py tests/__init__.py

echo "Project '$PROJECT_NAME' created and ready."

faq.question: Should I commit .venv to Git?
faq.answer: No. Add .venv to .gitignore and commit requirements.txt instead. Each developer creates their own .venv locally using pip install -r requirements.txt.

faq.question: What is the difference between venv and virtualenv?
faq.answer: venv is built into Python 3.3+ and is the recommended tool. virtualenv is a third-party package with extra features like support for Python 2, but for most projects venv is sufficient.

faq.question: How do I deactivate the virtual environment?
faq.answer: Run the 'deactivate' command in your terminal. This restores your shell to use the system Python and pip.
